Title#1: "Me and the U.P." autobiography of E. Deane Miller
Abstract#1: Autobiography written and illustrated by Elmer Deane Miller. It describes the years of his life between 1901-1970 and includes topics such as his childhood in the Midwest, attending dental school, performing in bands, marriage, and his love for the Union Pacific Railroad. There are many ink drawings throughout the document. The autobiography also contains pictures of Deane Miller.

Title#1: Correspondence from Don Haynes to George Simon
Abstract#1: Handwritten letter from Don Haynes to George Simon. Haynes apologizes for the late letter since he's been busy after Glenn Miller's disappearance in December 1944. Despite rumors, Haynes admits there have been no sightings of the plane or any of the passengers. He continues to talk about the orders he received from the Air Force and his duty to carry out Miller's musical touring. He also admits while Paul Dudley and other members of the band have done a great job with production, it's not the same without having Glenn at the front of the band. He ends the letter with a brief comment about how terrific Paris is in the springtime.

Title#1: Correspondence from Don Haynes to George Simon
Abstract#1: Handwritten letter from Don Haynes to George Simon. Haynes tells Simon that the U.S. Army Air Forces Band just returned from a twelve-day tour in the Riviera. They entertained thousands of U.S. troops who had returned from combat. Haynes also admits he will miss the opportunity to participate in "Major Glenn Miller Day" but will attend in spirit. He then lists the titles of several "old favorite" songs performed by Glenn Miller's Orchestra before the war. He ends the letter by stating the band will perform songs in Germany before returning to the U.S.

Title#1: Correspondence from Don Haynes to George Simon
Abstract#1: Handwritten letter from Don Haynes to George Simon. Haynes encloses the program from the gala performance by the U.S. Army Air Forces Band benefiting the French Prisoners of War Relief and describes the various groups of people who attended the event. He then shifts to the band's upcoming six-week tour of Germany, where they will perform for Army personnel. Haynes notes that their first performance will take place at Nuremberg Stadium, remarking that although Adolf Hitler once held rallies there, it is one of the few structures left standing in the city after the war. He excitedly shares that the tour will conclude on July 21, 1945, after which the band will return to the United States. Haynes closes the letter by saying he has not received news about Glenn Miller's fate following his disappearance. Reflecting on Miller's absence, he admits he is feeling sentimental as he thinks about the joy Miller's music brought to so many people.
Subject Geographic#1: Nuremberg (Bavaria, Germany, Europe) (inhabited place)

Title#1: Correspondence from George Simon to Glenn Miller
Abstract#1: A typed letter from George Simon to Glenn Miller. Simon admits that he was disappointed about being left out of Miller's plans for the U.S. Army Air Forces Band. Despite feeling like he didn't belong, Simon found other ways to get involved with the war effort. He talks about his experience working with NBC to book talent for a radio show called, "For the Record." Simon also comments on Miller's frustration regarding criticism of Miller’s band in a Metronome magazine article. Simon tells Miller that he's receiving great news about the band from folks like Dinah, Bing, and Spike Jones. He asks Miller if there is a way he can record one of his concerts while performing abroad. He finishes the letter by stating Miller can reach out to him for anything.

Title#1: Correspondence from Glenn Miller to George Simon
Abstract#1: A typed letter from Glenn Miller to George Simon. Miller tells Simon he is interested in running an advertisement in Metronome magazine for fifteen dollars per month. He mentions that if the ad runs with Rockwell O'Keefe it would cost at least ten dollars per month. He instructs Simon to be sure Metronome spells "Glenn" correctly, that the ad states "Brunswick Records," and that several hotels are opening. Miller finishes the letter by stating a man named Faz is joining the band, with more members to follow soon.

Title#1: Correspondence from Glenn Miller to George Simon
Abstract#1: Typed letter from Glenn Miller to George Simon. Miller updates Simon on several recent additions to the orchestra, including Faz on clarinet, a new drummer, new cornet players, and a front line of saxophonists. He thanks Simon for his continued interest in the orchestra and asks whether he could arrange for Mrs. Le Brosi to come hear the band perform. Miller closes the letter with a postscript noting that Les Beigle has organized a small six-piece Dixieland band.

Title#1: Correspondence from Glenn Miller to George Simon
Abstract#1: A typed letter from Glenn Miller to George Simon. Miller tells Simon he is happy to know that Simon is part of the "1-A boys" and will try his best to get Simon assigned to Miller's unit in the U.S. Army Air Forces Band. Miller also tells Simon there is no way a man named Buddy Weed would be transferred into the Air Corps but asks Simon to let him know if he ever does get transferred.

Title#1: Correspondence from Glenn Miller to George Simon
Abstract#1: A typed letter from Glenn Miller to George Simon. Miller tells Simon he has been busy touring with the U.S. Army Air Forces Band. The band attended thirty-five bases and forty-four broadcasts in order to perform for troops during the war. Miller admits he doesn't appreciate the criticism presented in an article in Metronome magazine. Miller sets the record straight that the band is performing only to bring a touch of home to troops during the war, rather than start a new style of swing music, as the article asserted. Miller continues to state that the critic knows nothing about technical aspects involved with music and misunderstood the conditions the band endures while playing abroad.

Title#1: Correspondence from Helen and Glenn Miller to George Simon
Abstract#1: yped letter from Helen and Glenn Miller to George T. Simon. Helen explains that Glenn has several things he wants to tell Simon but has not had time to write, so she is writing on his behalf. She says Glenn enjoyed the comments Simon included in his previous telegrams and agreed with his observations about the Brunswick records. Helen also notes Glenn's concerns about the "drum and cornet situation" and explains that a young musician from Denver, who replaced Jimmy Troutman in the band, has been a disappointment. She goes on to discuss planned additions and personnel changes within the band. Before mailing the letter, Helen asks Glenn to add a brief handwritten note to Simon.

Title#1: The Glenn Miller Orchestra in Hollywood filming "Orchestra Wives"
Abstract#1: Behind-the-scenes footage filmed by Paul Tanner of the Glenn Miller Orchestra in Hollywood during the production of "Orchestra Wives."
Note#1: 00:00:00 Footage of presumably George Montgomery walking towards the camera. 00:00:18 Behind the scenes footage from the shooting of the film Orchestra Wives. Actors identified: Cesar Romero, George Montgomery, Jackie Gleason, Ann Rutherford, Glenn Miller, Lynn Bari, Mary Beth Hughes, Virginia Gilmore, and Carole Landis.00:07:04 Behind the scenes footage of an unknown film scene featuring an outdoor above ground water set up with a skyline backdrop and fake boats. 00:07:39 More footage of the behind the scenes of Orchestra Wives and the unidentified project. 00:09:18 Footage of the streets of Los Angeles. Landmarks: The Brown Derby restaurant on Wilshire Blvd, CBS, Ciro's, the Earl Carroll Theatre, Mocambo, and Gilmore Stadium. 00:10:28 Scenes inside Gilmore Stadium of a parade and the crowd. 00:13:38 Footage of someone opening an envelope addressed to Paul Tanner with an invitation to the Second Annual Dinner Dance at the Biltmore Bowl. 00:13:57 Footage of presumably the Second Annual Dinner Dance at the Biltmore Bowl where people are singing and dancing on a stage. 00:14:53 Footage of an unidentified house. 00:15:20 End of recording.

Title#1: The Glenn Miller Orchestra in Hollywood, New York, and Ohio
Abstract#1: Behind-the-scenes footage filmed by Paul Tanner of the Glenn Miller Orchestra in Hollywood during the production of "Sun Valley Serenade." Additional footage includes Theater marquees, Marion Hutton's shower, footage of the Tanner's family, and a farm in Columbus, Ohio.
Note#1: 00:00:00 Behind the scenes footage of the Glenn Miller Orchestra on the set of Sun Valley Serenade. 00:02:52 Behind the scenes footage of Sonja Henie and John Payne [or a student double] performing an ice-skating/dancing scene. 00:05:11 More behind the scenes footage of Sonja Henie, John Payne, other unidentified actors, and the Glenn Miller Orchestra. 00:13:02 Footage of the exterior marquees of the Hotel Pennsylvania, the Paramount Theater, The Shubert Theater, the RKO Palace, and the Fox Theatre in New York interspersed with footage of presumably the Glenn Miller Orchestra members. 00:15:59 Footage driving down a highway in presumably Columbus, Ohio. 00:16:13 Footage of Marion Hutton's baby shower. 00:18:16 Footage of an unidentified man and dog walking around a forest in winter. 00:18:41 Footage of presumably Paul Tanner's family. 00:19:45 End of recording.

Title#1: The Glenn Miller Orchestra on the road and various performances
Abstract#1: Behind-the-scenes footage filmed by Paul Tanner of the Glenn Miller Orchestra traveling and performing.
Note#1: 00:00:00 Footage of an unidentified man playing darts, and unidentified man writing at a desk, and the Glenn Miller Orchestra traveling. 00:00:59 Footage of the exterior marquee of the Palace Theatre and the Glenn Miller Orchestra performing. 00:05:01 Footage of unidentified performers getting ready for a show and signing autographs. 00:06:06 Footage of the Fox Theatre, the Palace Theatre, The Forum Theatre, The Gaiety Theatre, and other theatre marquees publicizing the Glenn Miller Orchestra, stage entrance signs, the Glenn Miller Orchestra backstage and performing, and stage scheduling signage and paperwork. 00:09:36 The Glenn Miller band traveling, swimming and diving, and performing. 00:13:10 Unidentified people making funny faces and doing an impression of either Charlie Chaplin or Hitler. 00:13:56 Footage of members of the orchestra walking on the street, tuning their instruments, signing autographs, preparing for the show, and performing. 00:18:11 End of recording.
Note#2: [Cataloger's note: An unidentified person in this film performs an impersonation of either Charlie Chaplin or Adolf Hitler. It is difficult to discern based on the time period in which the film was created and the mannerisms of the impersonation. That being said, in 1940, Charlie Chaplin wrote, directed, and starred in a satire titled "The Great Dictator." Considering this Glenn Miller film is documenting the behind-the-scenes life of musicians and actors it is likely that the impersonation is of Charlie Chaplin impersonating Hitler.]

Title#1: Walter and Virginia Newton's dance scrapbook
Abstract#1: A scrapbook containing various memorabilia related to Walter "Clicker" Newton and Virginia "Ginny" Folts who were New York City "shag" dance champions. The book includes photographs, newspaper clippings, correspondence, dance medals, and programs between 1939-1940. Additional materials from the late 1990s and early 2000s include the Newtons' participation with the Glenn Miller Festival and Glenn Miller Birthplace Society.
